nodes.name: Famous Artists
nodes.description: Major visual artists from the Renaissance to modern day

items.prompt: Which Italian Renaissance polymath, born in 1452, painted the *Mona Lisa* and *The Last Supper*, filled notebooks with anatomical drawings and inventions, and famously wrote in mirror script with his left hand?
items.explanation:
Italian, 1452–1519, born in Vinci near Florence
Painted the *Mona Lisa* (Louvre) and *The Last Supper* (Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan) — two of the most famous paintings in history
Filled thousands of notebook pages with anatomical studies, flying machines, and engineering designs, centuries ahead of their time
Left-handed; wrote in mirror script (right to left) that required a mirror to read
Worked as painter, sculptor, architect, engineer, and scientist — the archetypal Renaissance man
Rival of the younger Michelangelo; the two despised each other in Florence
Died at Amboise, France, in the arms of King Francis I according to legend (probably apocryphal)
Only about 15–20 surviving paintings are attributed to him

items.prompt: Which Italian Renaissance artist painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel, sculpted the 17-foot marble *David* in Florence, and designed the dome of St. Peter's Basilica in Rome?
items.explanation:
Italian, 1475–1564, born in Caprese near Arezzo
Painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling (1508–1512) including *The Creation of Adam* — painted lying on scaffolding, not on his back as myth claims
Sculpted *David* (1501–1504) from a marble block two other sculptors had rejected
*Pietà* (1499), carved at age 24, is the only work he ever signed — he chiseled his name across the Madonna's sash
Appointed chief architect of St. Peter's Basilica in 1547; designed its massive dome
Rival of Leonardo da Vinci; once mocked Leonardo publicly in Florence for failing to finish a horse statue
Wrote over 300 poems, many homoerotic, addressed to the nobleman Tommaso dei Cavalieri
Died at 88, working until six days before his death

items.prompt: Which Dutch Post-Impressionist painter, who sold only one painting during his lifetime, created *The Starry Night* and cut off part of his own ear in 1888 before dying of a self-inflicted gunshot wound at age 37?
items.explanation:
Dutch, 1853–1890, born in Groot-Zundert, Netherlands
Post-Impressionist known for bold color, emotional intensity, and thick impasto brushwork
*The Starry Night* (1889) painted from the window of his room at the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole asylum in Saint-Rémy
Cut off part of his left ear (likely just the lobe) in December 1888 after a confrontation with Paul Gauguin in Arles
Sold only one painting in his lifetime: *The Red Vineyard* (1888), for 400 francs
Shot himself in the chest in a wheat field at Auvers-sur-Oise on July 27, 1890; died two days later at 37
Produced about 2,100 artworks in roughly a decade, including ~860 oil paintings
His brother Theo was his lifelong financial supporter and confidant; their letters are a major art-historical document

items.prompt: Which Spanish artist co-founded Cubism with Georges Braque, painted the anti-war mural *Guernica*, went through Blue and Rose periods, and is estimated to have produced around 50,000 works in his lifetime?
items.explanation:
Spanish, 1881–1973, born in Málaga, Spain
Co-founded Cubism with Georges Braque around 1907–1908, shattering traditional perspective
*Les Demoiselles d'Avignon* (1907) is considered the most revolutionary painting of the 20th century
*Guernica* (1937) depicts the Nazi bombing of a Basque town during the Spanish Civil War
Blue Period (1901–1904) and Rose Period (1904–1906) preceded his Cubist breakthrough
Most prolific artist ever recorded: an estimated 50,000 works including paintings, sculptures, prints, and ceramics
Lived in France for most of his adult life; refused to return to Spain under Franco's dictatorship
Died at 91 in Mougins, France; his estate took six years to inventory

items.prompt: Which French painter's 1872 work *Impression, Sunrise* gave the Impressionist movement its name, and who spent decades painting water lilies in the garden he designed at his home in Giverny?
items.explanation:
French, 1840–1926, born in Paris, raised in Le Havre
*Impression, Sunrise* (1872) was ridiculed by a critic who coined the term 'Impressionism' as an insult
Painted approximately 250 *Water Lilies* canvases over the last 30 years of his life
Designed the famous water garden at Giverny with the Japanese bridge, which became his primary subject
Pioneered painting en plein air (outdoors) to capture changing light conditions
Often painted the same subject at different times of day: haystacks, Rouen Cathedral, the Houses of Parliament
Developed cataracts in his 70s that shifted his color perception; late paintings became increasingly abstract and reddish
Died at 86 in Giverny; buried in the local churchyard

items.prompt: Which Dutch Golden Age painter, famous for *The Night Watch* and an extraordinary series of roughly 80 self-portraits spanning his career, mastered the use of light and shadow but went bankrupt in 1656?
items.explanation:
Dutch, 1606–1669, born in Leiden, worked primarily in Amsterdam
*The Night Watch* (1642) is the most famous Dutch painting; actually depicts a daytime scene (dark varnish obscured the original colors)
Painted roughly 80 self-portraits over 40 years — the most sustained self-examination by any artist before photography
Master of chiaroscuro: dramatic contrast of light and dark, especially in his portraits and biblical scenes
Declared bankrupt in 1656; his house and art collection were auctioned off
Ran the largest painting workshop in the Netherlands; questions of attribution persist to this day
His son Titus and common-law wife Hendrickje Stoffels both died before him
Died in poverty in 1669 and was buried in an unmarked grave in Amsterdam's Westerkerk

items.prompt: Which Mexican artist, known for vivid self-portraits incorporating elements of surrealism and Mexican folk art, survived a devastating bus accident at 18 and had a turbulent marriage to muralist Diego Rivera?
items.explanation:
Mexican, 1907–1954, born and died in Coyoacán, Mexico City (in the Blue House, now a museum)
A bus accident at age 18 shattered her spine, collarbone, ribs, and pelvis; she endured 30+ surgeries and chronic pain for life
55 of her 143 paintings are self-portraits — 'I paint myself because I am so often alone'
Married muralist Diego Rivera twice (1929 and 1940); both had numerous affairs, including Kahlo's with Leon Trotsky
Surrealist André Breton claimed her as a Surrealist, but she rejected the label: 'I never painted dreams. I painted my own reality'
Her unibrow and traditional Tehuana dress became feminist and cultural icons
Died at 47; official cause was pulmonary embolism, though some suspect suicide by overdose
Her work was largely forgotten until a feminist revival in the 1970s–80s; now among the most expensive by any female artist

items.prompt: Which Spanish Surrealist, famous for his flamboyant handlebar mustache, painted *The Persistence of Memory* with its melting clocks and collaborated with filmmaker Luis Buñuel on the short film *Un Chien Andalou*?
items.explanation:
Spanish, 1904–1989, born in Figueres, Catalonia
*The Persistence of Memory* (1931) with its melting clocks is one of the most recognizable paintings in the world (MoMA, New York)
Collaborated with Luis Buñuel on the Surrealist films *Un Chien Andalou* (1929) and *L'Âge d'Or* (1930)
Designed the dream sequence for Hitchcock's *Spellbound* (1945) and the Chupa Chups lollipop logo
His trademark upturned handlebar mustache was inspired by 17th-century painter Diego Velázquez
Expelled from the Surrealist movement by André Breton for his fascist sympathies and self-promotion
Created the Dalí Theatre-Museum in Figueres, where he is buried in a crypt beneath the stage
In 2017 his body was exhumed for a paternity suit; the claim was dismissed, but his mustache was reportedly still intact

items.prompt: Which American artist, a leading figure of Pop Art, became famous for his silkscreened images of Campbell's Soup cans and Marilyn Monroe, ran a studio called The Factory, and was shot by Valerie Solanas in 1968?
items.explanation:
American, 1928–1987, born Andrew Warhola in Pittsburgh to Slovak immigrant parents
Pioneered Pop Art by elevating commercial imagery: Campbell's Soup Cans (1962), Brillo Boxes, Coca-Cola bottles
Marilyn Diptych (1962) — silkscreen prints of Monroe became icons of 20th-century art
Coined (or popularized) the phrase 'In the future, everyone will be world-famous for 15 minutes'
Ran The Factory, his Manhattan studio that became a hangout for musicians, drag queens, and the Velvet Underground
Shot nearly fatally by radical feminist Valerie Solanas on June 3, 1968; spent weeks in critical condition and wore a surgical corset for life
Also a filmmaker: directed over 60 experimental films including *Chelsea Girls* (1966)
Died at 58 from cardiac arrhythmia after gallbladder surgery; his estate was worth ~$600 million
items.cardFront: Pop Art, Campbell's Soup, The Factory

items.prompt: Which Austrian artist, leader of the Vienna Secession movement, is best known for *The Kiss* and his distinctive use of gold leaf, and whose erotic paintings scandalized early 20th-century Vienna?
items.explanation:
Austrian, 1862–1918, born in Baumgarten near Vienna
*The Kiss* (1907–1908) is his most famous work, depicting an embracing couple wrapped in gold-patterned robes (Österreichische Galerie Belvedere, Vienna)
Co-founded the Vienna Secession (1897), an Art Nouveau movement that broke from conservative academic art
Gold leaf technique in his 'Golden Phase' was inspired by Byzantine mosaics he saw in Ravenna, Italy
His ceiling paintings for the University of Vienna were attacked as 'pornographic' and ultimately destroyed in a fire during WWII
*Portrait of Adele Bloch-Bauer I* (1907) sold for $135 million in 2006 — then the most expensive painting ever; its Nazi-era theft was depicted in the film *Woman in Gold* (2015)
Never married but fathered an estimated 14 children; kept numerous mistresses
Died in 1918 during the Spanish flu pandemic, from a stroke followed by pneumonia

items.prompt: Which American Abstract Expressionist, nicknamed 'Jack the Dripper,' pioneered the drip painting technique by pouring and flinging paint onto canvases laid on the floor, and died in an alcohol-related car crash at 44?
items.explanation:
American, 1912–1956, born in Cody, Wyoming
Pioneered drip painting (action painting): laid canvases on the floor and poured, dripped, and flung house paint using sticks and hardened brushes
Nicknamed 'Jack the Dripper' by *Time* magazine in 1956
*No. 5, 1948* reportedly sold for $140 million in 2006, one of the most expensive paintings ever
The CIA secretly promoted Abstract Expressionism (including Pollock) during the Cold War as a weapon of cultural freedom against Soviet realism
Struggled with alcoholism throughout his life; underwent Jungian psychotherapy
Died on August 11, 1956, in a drunk-driving crash in Springs, New York, at age 44; one of his two passengers also died
His wife, Lee Krasner, was also a significant Abstract Expressionist painter; she managed his estate after his death

items.prompt: Which American artist, called the 'Mother of American Modernism,' is celebrated for her large-scale flower paintings and New Mexico desert landscapes, was married to photographer Alfred Stieglitz, and lived to age 98?
items.explanation:
American, 1887–1986, born in Sun Prairie, Wisconsin
Called the 'Mother of American Modernism' for her pioneering abstract and semi-abstract work starting in the 1910s
Large-scale flower paintings (*Jimson Weed/White Flower No. 1*, 1932) are her most iconic works; she rejected Freudian sexual interpretations
Moved to New Mexico permanently in 1949; painted its landscapes, animal skulls, and adobe architecture for decades
Married photographer and gallery owner Alfred Stieglitz in 1924; he exhibited her work and took hundreds of photographs of her
*Jimson Weed/White Flower No. 1* sold for $44.4 million in 2014 — then a record for any female artist at auction
Lived to 98, continuing to work even after losing central vision in the 1970s; switched to pottery and clay
Received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1977

items.prompt: Which anonymous British street artist, whose identity remains unconfirmed, is known for satirical political stencil art and made headlines when *Girl with Balloon* self-shredded moments after selling at Sotheby's for £1 million in 2018?
items.explanation:
British (believed from Bristol), identity unknown, active since the early 1990s
*Girl with Balloon* (2018) partially shredded itself via a mechanism hidden in the frame moments after selling at Sotheby's for £1.04 million; renamed *Love is in the Bin*, it later resold for £18.6 million in 2021
Stencil-based street art with political and social commentary: anti-war, anti-capitalism, pro-refugee themes
*Dismaland* (2015) was a dystopian parody of Disneyland in Weston-super-Mare, England
Opened the Walled Off Hotel in Bethlehem (2017) overlooking the Israeli West Bank barrier, billed as the 'worst view in the world'
His film *Exit Through the Gift Shop* (2010) was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Documentary
Multiple candidates for his identity have been proposed, most commonly Robin Gunningham from Bristol
Works sell for millions despite (or because of) his anonymity; originals on buildings are sometimes cut from walls and auctioned

items.prompt: Which Norwegian Expressionist painter created *The Scream*, a work depicting an agonized figure against a tumultuous sky, which has been stolen from museums twice — in 1994 and again in 2004?
items.explanation:
Norwegian, 1863–1944, born in Ådal, Norway
*The Scream* (1893) — actually a series of four versions (two paintings, two pastels) — depicts existential anxiety, not a person screaming but hearing 'a scream through nature'
Stolen from the National Gallery in Oslo in 1994 (on the opening day of the Winter Olympics in Lillehammer); recovered three months later
A different version stolen from the Munch Museum in 2004 by armed robbers; recovered in 2006
A pastel version sold at Sotheby's in 2012 for nearly $120 million, then a record for any artwork at auction
Part of a larger series called *The Frieze of Life*, exploring love, anxiety, and death
His mother and favorite sister both died of tuberculosis when he was young, deeply shaping his art's themes of illness and mortality
Donated his entire remaining collection (over 1,000 paintings, 15,000 prints) to the city of Oslo

items.prompt: Which Italian Baroque painter, born Michelangelo Merisi, revolutionized art with his dramatic use of chiaroscuro called tenebrism, but killed a man in a brawl in 1606 and spent his final years as a fugitive?
items.explanation:
Italian, 1571–1610, born Michelangelo Merisi in Milan (named after the town of Caravaggio)
Pioneered tenebrism: extreme contrast between deep shadow and bright illumination, giving scenes theatrical drama
Used ordinary people as models for saints and biblical figures — shocking for the Counter-Reformation era
Killed Ranuccio Tomassoni in a street brawl in Rome in 1606; fled and never returned
Spent his last four years as a fugitive in Naples, Malta, and Sicily, still producing masterpieces
Knighted by the Order of Malta in 1608, then expelled after another violent incident
Died under mysterious circumstances at age 38 on a beach in Porto Ercole, possibly of fever, lead poisoning, or murder
Lost and forgotten for centuries; rediscovered by art historians in the early 20th century

items.prompt: Which French artist led the Fauvist movement with his bold, non-naturalistic use of color, became a rival and close friend of Picasso, and turned to paper cut-outs in his later years when he could no longer paint due to illness?
items.explanation:
French, 1869–1954, born in Le Cateau-Cambrésis, northern France
Leader of Fauvism ('wild beasts') — a movement characterized by bold, arbitrary color; *Woman with a Hat* (1905) scandalized the Salon d'Automne
*The Dance* (1910) and *The Red Studio* (1911) are among his most celebrated works
Rival and friend of Picasso for over 40 years; they exchanged paintings and Picasso kept Matisse's works after his death
After cancer surgery in 1941 left him largely bedridden, he invented the 'cut-out' technique — painting with scissors on colored paper
The cut-outs, including *The Snail* and *Blue Nudes*, are now considered his crowning achievement
Designed the entire interior of the Chapelle du Rosaire in Vence, France (1948–1951), including stained glass, murals, and vestments
Died in 1954 in Nice; Picasso said 'Matisse has always been the only one'

items.prompt: Which Dutch Golden Age painter, active in Delft, created *Girl with a Pearl Earring* and is known for luminous interior scenes, yet left only about 35 confirmed paintings and may have used a camera obscura?
items.explanation:
Dutch, 1632–1675, born and worked in Delft
*Girl with a Pearl Earring* (c. 1665) is called the 'Mona Lisa of the North' (Mauritshuis, The Hague)
Only about 34–36 paintings are attributed to him — one of the smallest known œuvres of any major artist
Master of light: his interior scenes feature a distinctive soft, pearly luminosity, often with light streaming from a left-side window
The camera obscura theory suggests he used an optical device to achieve his photographic precision; debated but not proven
Virtually forgotten for nearly 200 years until rediscovered by critic Théophile Thoré-Bürger in the 1860s
Han van Meegeren forged several 'Vermeers' in the 1930s–40s, fooling museums and even Hermann Göring
Died at 43, likely of stress-related illness, leaving his wife and 11 children in severe debt

items.prompt: Which American neo-expressionist artist began as a graffiti artist under the tag SAMO in New York, collaborated with Andy Warhol, became the first major Black American artist in mainstream galleries, and died of a heroin overdose at 27?
items.explanation:
American, 1960–1988, born in Brooklyn, New York, of Haitian and Puerto Rican descent
Began as a graffiti artist spraying cryptic aphorisms under the tag SAMO (©) on buildings in Lower Manhattan
First major Black American artist to achieve mainstream gallery and auction success, breaking racial barriers in the art world
Collaborated with Andy Warhol on a series of joint paintings in 1984–1985; their friendship was complex and competitive
Work combines text, symbols, anatomy, and references to Black history, jazz, and racism — often on raw, unprimed canvas
His painting *Untitled* (1982) — a skull-like head — sold for $110.5 million at Sotheby's in 2017 to Japanese collector Yusaku Maezawa
Died of a heroin overdose on August 12, 1988, at age 27, joining the so-called '27 Club'
Had no formal art training; dropped out of high school at 17
